narrative:
Matt Van Epps has been in Congress since December 2025, which in Washington terms makes him roughly the age of a fruit fly. A West Point graduate, Apache and Chinook pilot, Bronze Star recipient, and former 160th Special Operations Aviation Regiment air mission commander, Van Epps arrived on Capitol Hill with the kind of resume that makes you think: finally, someone who might actually hold the Pentagon accountable. Instead, he joined the Homeland Security Committee, the Republican Study Committee, and immediately set about doing exactly what his billionaire backers paid for.

And they paid handsomely. Van Epps' special election campaign was a masterclass in dark money theater. Club for Growth — bankrolled by Jeff Yass and Virginia James — funneled roughly $850,000 through the School Freedom Fund to boost his primary. Conservatives for American Excellence, fueled by hedge fund titans Ken Griffin and Paul Singer, dropped another $600,000. MAGA Inc. piled on. All told, Super PACs spent over $7 million in the TN-7 special election. Van Epps himself pulled in nearly $1 million, with PAC money outpacing his opponent Aftyn Behn's PAC haul by a ratio of six to one. For a self-described "fiscal hawk," he certainly has no problem letting billionaires foot his campaign bills.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Van Epps presents a fascinating case study in saying one thing and funding another. He signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge — a brave stance from a man who has held office for approximately four months. He champions himself as a budget hawk who wants a Balanced Budget Amendment and an end to wasteful omnibus spending, yet he enthusiastically supported the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, a reconciliation package estimated to add $3 trillion to the national debt over ten years. Apparently "fiscal responsibility" means something different when the tax cuts flow to the ultra-wealthy and their corporate vehicles.

On stock trading bans, lobbying reform, Pentagon audits, Medicare drug negotiation, the DISCLOSE Act, banning corporate PACs, and the Epstein files? Silence so thick you could cut it with a Ka-Bar. The man who flew combat missions tracking high-value targets somehow cannot locate a single position on whether Congress members should be allowed to trade stocks on inside information. His committee assignments — Homeland Security and Science, Space, and Technology — conveniently keep him far from the Financial Services or Ways and Means committees where these fights actually happen.

Van Epps talks about "draining the swamp" while being carried into office on the shoulders of the swamp's wealthiest alligators. Tennessee's 7th District deserves better than a decorated veteran who traded his combat gear for a PAC-funded suit and a seat at the establishment table.

Maxine Waters has represented South Los Angeles in Congress since 1991, making her one of the longest-serving members of the House and, as ranking member of the Financial Services Committee, one of the most powerful Democrats in Washington when it comes to banking, housing, and Wall Street regulation [1]. She is known for a combative style -- "Auntie Maxine" to her fans, "reclaiming my time" to the culture -- and a career built on championing the poor, the unhoused, and communities of color in one of the most economically stratified cities in America [2]. She is also, as her own Federal Election Commission file attests, a congresswoman whose personal financial arrangements have a way of contradicting her populist brand.

On the substance of the 80% Bill, Waters has genuine credentials. She voted for the Inflation Reduction Act and its Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, albeit after publicly threatening to tank the bill because it stripped out $150 billion in housing spending she had fought for -- a protest that, whatever its political calculation, at least showed she was paying attention to what got cut [3][4]. She supported the Build Back Better Act with its paid family leave provisions. She championed the For the People Act and its anti-gerrymandering and campaign disclosure requirements. She has been a vocal critic of Citizens United and a supporter of the DISCLOSE Act [5]. She backed the repeal of the 2002 Iraq AUMF. And in March 2026, she reintroduced legislation to prohibit stock sales by senior bank executives when their institutions are under regulatory duress -- a narrow but meaningful reform aimed at the revolving door between Wall Street corner offices and Washington [6]. On the core economic populism of the 80% Bill -- taxing the ultra-wealthy, funding Social Security, banning junk fees -- Waters is a reliable yes vote.

The trouble is that Waters has a decades-long habit of generating the exact kind of conflicts of interest the 80% Bill is designed to eliminate. The most infamous episode involved OneUnited Bank, a minority-owned institution in which her husband Sidney Williams held stock worth over $350,000. In 2008, Waters arranged a meeting between OneUnited executives and Treasury officials; the bank subsequently received a $12.1 million TARP bailout [7]. The House Ethics Committee charged her with three violations, alleging she stood to benefit directly from the intervention. She was ultimately cleared in 2012, with investigators concluding she believed she was acting on behalf of all minority-owned banks [8]. The exoneration was real, but so was the optic: the ranking member of Financial Services, whose husband held six figures in bank stock, personally arranging a Treasury meeting for that bank during a financial crisis. It is the kind of arrangement that makes the case for a stock trading ban better than any advocacy group ever could.

Then there is the family business. Waters' daughter Karen has collected over $1.2 million from her mother's campaign committee since 2004 for running a "slate mailer" operation -- endorsement mailers sent to Los Angeles voters listing candidates who pay to appear on them [9]. The FEC blessed the arrangement, and the practice is common in California state politics, but Waters appears to be the only federal politician running one at this scale. In May 2025, the FEC fined Waters' campaign $68,000 for accepting excessive contributions, making prohibited cash disbursements, and failing to accurately report financial activity during the 2020 cycle [10][11]. Her campaign called the violations "unintentional." The supermajority might call them illustrative. Waters' net worth has climbed to an estimated $3.7 million, anchored by a $2 million Hancock Park mansion and multiple mortgaged properties [12]. None of this is illegal. All of it is the kind of self-dealing ecosystem that the 80% Bill's provisions on lobbying bans, stock trading restrictions, and campaign finance transparency are meant to dismantle.

Waters has spent 35 years in Congress telling Wall Street to its face that she is coming for it, and she has the legislative record to back up much of that rhetoric. But she has also spent 35 years demonstrating why structural reforms -- term limits, stock trading bans, campaign finance overhauls -- cannot depend on the goodwill of the people they are supposed to constrain. She is the rare member who genuinely supports most of the 80% Bill's agenda and simultaneously embodies the need for it.

Roger Wicker has represented Mississippi in Congress since 1995 -- first in the House, then in the Senate since 2007 -- making him one of the most durable Republican fixtures in Southern politics. As chairman of the Senate Armed Services Committee, Wicker has positioned himself as the chamber's foremost defense hawk, pushing to raise military spending to 5% of GDP and surpass $1 trillion annually [1][2]. He is also, famously, the only United States senator to vote against a 2015 amendment declaring that "climate change is real and not a hoax," losing 98-1 in what may be the loneliest roll call in Senate history [3].

On the 80% Bill, Wicker's record is a masterclass in opposition to popular policy. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation -- a provision supported by roughly 80% of Americans [4]. He went further, signing a letter with 23 Republican senators warning that allowing Medicare to negotiate drug prices would "undermine medical innovation" and co-sponsoring legislation to repeal the negotiation program entirely [5]. He voted to block a $35 monthly insulin price cap for private insurance, siding with pharmaceutical companies over the 7.4 million Americans who use insulin daily [6]. On campaign finance, Senate Republicans unanimously blocked the DISCLOSE Act in 2022, with the vote failing 49-49 on party lines -- Wicker included [7]. He has shown no interest in banning congressional stock trading, corporate PACs, or dark money, nor has he supported term limits, paid family leave legislation, or a lifetime lobbying ban.

Wicker has, to his credit, made noise about Pentagon accountability. In his defense reconciliation bill, he included $400 million for fiscal responsibility and a clean audit, requiring the Department of Defense to finally pass one [8]. But this is a bit like a restaurant critic demanding a health inspection of a kitchen he personally keeps fully stocked -- Wicker has spent his career ensuring the Pentagon gets every dollar it asks for and then some, proposing budgets that would make even some defense hawks blush.

The money tells the rest of the story. In the 2019-2024 cycle, Wicker's campaign was funded 32% by PAC contributions and 48% by large individual donors, with small donors contributing under 6% [9]. His top industry contributors include defense electronics, miscellaneous defense firms, and sea transport -- led by Huntington Ingalls Industries, the Pascagoula shipbuilder for whom Wicker has personally secured billions in Navy contracts [10][11]. He has also received substantial support from the oil and gas industry and pharmaceutical manufacturers, the very sectors that benefit most from his opposition to climate action and drug price negotiation [12][13]. Lobbyists have contributed generously throughout his career, a fact that makes his silence on lobbying reform less mysterious than it might otherwise appear [14].

Roger Wicker is, in short, a senator whose voting record reads like a wish list drafted by his donor base. He opposes nearly every item on the 80% Bill -- drug price negotiation, campaign finance reform, dark money disclosure, stock trading bans -- while championing unlimited defense spending for the contractors who fund his campaigns. Mississippians may love him, but the 80% of Americans who support these reforms might wonder whose interests he is actually serving.

Ron Wyden is the senior senator from Oregon and a man who has spent three decades in the Senate perfecting the art of being almost exactly right about everything while his family portfolio quietly beats the market by a factor of five. He is the walking embodiment of the gap between what Congress says and what Congress does — which is to say, he is the 80% Bill's most instructive case study.

Start with the good, because there is genuinely a lot of it. As ranking member of the Senate Finance Committee, Wyden authored the Billionaires Income Tax Act, which would impose mark-to-market taxation on unrealized capital gains for anyone worth over $1 billion. The Penn Wharton Budget Model projected it would raise $507 billion over ten years. He introduced the Treat Wealth Like Wages Act to close the capital gains loophole and shore up Social Security. Alongside Bernie Sanders, he introduced the Keep Billionaires Out of Social Security Act to reverse Trump-era SSA cuts. He wants the ultra-wealthy to pay their share, and he has the legislative text to prove it.

On the Epstein files, Wyden has been arguably the most aggressive investigator in Congress. He used his Finance Committee authority to launch a multi-year probe into Epstein's finances, uncovering over $1 billion in suspicious transactions. He introduced the PETRA Act to force Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to hand over Epstein-related records. When Bessent refused, Wyden expanded the investigation. When Senate Republicans blocked the bill in March 2026, he kept pushing. He also probed the IRS's failure to investigate Epstein's tax planning schemes. This is serious, sustained oversight work.

He co-sponsored the DISCLOSE Act to expose dark money donors, introduced legislation with Senators Merkley and Tester to overturn rules protecting anonymous political spending, and voted against Treasury's pro-dark money rule. On right to repair, he introduced the bipartisan Fair Repair Act with Senators Lummis and Lujan. On Pentagon accountability, he co-sponsored the Audit the Pentagon Act with Sanders, Grassley, and Lee, and voted against the NDAA when it failed to include audit requirements. On Medicare, he introduced the NOBBBA Act to block an $8.8 billion pharma bailout hidden in the Republican budget bill.

Now for the asterisk. In 2024, Wyden's family stock portfolio surged 123.8% — roughly five times the S&P 500's return and nearly ten times what elite hedge funds managed. His wife, Nancy Bass Wyden, holds up to $1 million each in Apple, Microsoft, and Nvidia stock, alongside significant Amazon and Google positions. She purchased semiconductor stocks while Wyden championed the CHIPS Act. Wyden maintains he and his wife keep their finances separate. Ethics experts maintain that this explanation is, to use the technical term, insufficient. Rolling Stone reported that Wyden has pushed Big Tech's interests while his wife holds millions in tech stocks. The Revolving Door Project called it an ethical conflict. Republicans called for his resignation from the Finance Committee.

Ron Wyden writes the best reform legislation in the Senate while his household portfolio makes it look like insider trading is a family hobby. He is the senator who proves you can be right about policy and compromised by practice simultaneously. His bills deserve to pass. His stock disclosures deserve a forensic audit.

Nikema Williams was chosen to succeed the late John Lewis on the ballot for Georgia's 5th Congressional District in July 2020, inheriting a seat synonymous with the civil rights movement and a set of expectations that would crush a lesser politician. The first Black woman to chair the Georgia Democratic Party, Williams had already built a reputation as a voting rights organizer before arriving in Congress in January 2021 [1]. She serves on the House Financial Services Committee, where she focuses on affordable housing and closing the racial wealth gap, and she co-chairs the Congressional Voting Rights Caucus, a role that keeps her squarely in the Lewis tradition [2]. In March 2025, she resigned as Georgia Democratic Party chair amid criticism that her dual role limited her fundraising effectiveness and that party resources had been mismanaged under her leadership [3].

On the 80% Bill issues, Williams is one of the more active members of the Georgia delegation. She co-led the End Dark Money Act with Rep. Jason Crow, legislation that would close loopholes allowing mega-donors to hide political contributions through "social welfare" organizations [4]. She is a cosponsor of the DISCLOSE Act, which would require transparency in election spending [5]. She presided over the House during debate of H.R. 4, the John Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act, and has introduced her own comprehensive voting rights package to address modern voter suppression tactics, aligning her with the 80% Bill's call for fair elections and an end to gerrymandering [6]. She voted yes on the Iran War Powers Resolution, signaling support for congressional reassertion of war-making authority [7]. On her campaign website, she lists paid family and medical leave, quality childcare, and long-term care as priorities she will fight to make a federal reality [8].

Williams sponsors legislation primarily in government operations and politics (28%), health (24%), and transportation (12%), according to GovTrack, and her missed-vote rate of 0.8% over her tenure is well below the House median of 2.1% [9]. She shows up, in other words, which in the current Congress qualifies as a distinguishing characteristic. Her Financial Services Committee seat, however, places her in the same donor ecosystem that has historically rewarded members of that panel with generous contributions from the finance, insurance, and real estate sector. OpenSecrets data shows her campaign finance profile, though the specific industry breakdowns deserve ongoing scrutiny for any member sitting on the committee that oversees Wall Street [10].

Williams has positioned herself as a reform-minded progressive carrying the moral weight of John Lewis's legacy. Her legislative record on dark money, voting rights, and election transparency is substantively stronger than many of her peers. The test, as always, is whether a Financial Services Committee member can champion the people's interests while the industries she regulates write the checks. Lewis managed it for decades. Whether his successor can do the same with a modern fundraising apparatus will be the story of her career.

Raphael Warnock is the junior United States senator from Georgia, the senior pastor of Atlanta's Ebenezer Baptist Church, and a man who won the most expensive Senate race of the 2022 cycle by raising over $150 million against Herschel Walker [1]. He is the first Black senator from Georgia, elected in a January 2021 runoff and re-elected in a December 2022 runoff, both times in contests that turned the state into a national ATM for Democratic donors. Warnock serves on the Senate Banking Committee, the Commerce Committee, and the Special Committee on Aging, and he has built a legislative brand around prescription drug costs and voting rights [2].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Warnock checks more boxes than most of his colleagues. He co-led the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act alongside fellow Georgia Senator Jon Ossoff, which would require members, spouses, and dependents to divest individual stock holdings or place them in a blind trust [3]. He joined 39 Democratic and Independent senators to reintroduce a constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United [4]. He cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act to require transparency in election spending [5]. His signature legislative achievement was authoring provisions in the Inflation Reduction Act that capped insulin costs at $35 a month for Medicare patients and limited seniors' out-of-pocket drug expenses to $2,000 per year, a concrete win on Medicare drug negotiation [6]. On war powers, he supported repealing the 1991 and 2002 Iraq AUMFs and publicly questioned the White House's military strategy after the Senate failed to pass a war powers resolution [7]. He has championed Social Security protections, introduced major affordable housing legislation, and backed the For the People Act's sweeping election reforms [8].

The money story, however, has a wrinkle. Warnock pledged never to take "a dime of corporate PAC money," but reporting by the Daily Caller and OpenSecrets found that his campaign accepted thousands from leadership PACs that were themselves heavily funded by corporate-backed PACs, including those connected to Amazon, AT&T, Pfizer, CVS, and the Carlyle Group [9]. His campaign also received $8,400 from corporate lobbyists in the first half of 2022 alone [10]. His fundraising geography tells its own story: he raised roughly $13.4 million from California, compared to $7.3 million from his home state of Georgia, with the entertainment industry contributing nearly $1 million [1]. For a senator who speaks the language of small-dollar, grassroots democracy, the donor base has a distinctly Hollywood zip code.

None of this erases Warnock's substantive legislative work, which on prescription drug pricing alone puts him ahead of most senators. But the gap between the "no corporate PAC money" pledge and the reality of leadership-PAC laundering is precisely the kind of loophole the 80% Bill is designed to close. Warnock talks a better game on reform than almost anyone in the Senate; whether the money behind him lets him play it is the question that matters.

Joe Wilson, the man who shouted "You lie!" at President Obama during a joint session of Congress, has spent over two decades in the House proving that decorum is optional but defense contractor money is mandatory. The senior member of the House Armed Services Committee represents South Carolina's 2nd District — home to Fort Jackson, the Army's largest initial entry training center — and has turned that geographic coincidence into a career-long justification for never questioning a Pentagon budget.

Wilson's district processes over 45,000 Army recruits annually and hosts billions in Department of Defense contracts. He has testified before the Budget Committee specifically to request more resources for Fort Jackson, championed every National Defense Authorization Act that crossed his desk, and advocated for a $640 billion-plus defense budget as a "Reaganite" believer in "peace through strength." Auditing the Pentagon? That would be like auditing his own campaign donors.

And about those donors: AIPAC is Wilson's all-time top contributor, pouring over $234,000 into his campaigns across recent cycles, with $133,000 in the 2024 cycle alone. His legislative director literally left his office to go work for AIPAC — the revolving door spinning so fast it generates its own lobbying gravity. Wilson has traveled to Israel twice on AIPAC's dime and has been the organization's reliable champion since his first term, when he compared a fellow senator to Saddam Hussein for voting against a pro-Israel resolution. End forever wars? Not while the checks keep clearing.

Wilson also joined 138 other House Republicans in objecting to Biden's 2020 electoral certification just hours after the January 6 Capitol breach. On the 80% Bill scorecard, he's a near-perfect zero: no support for banning stock trading, no interest in corporate PAC reform (he depends on them), no movement on dark money disclosure, no backing for Medicare drug negotiation, and no appetite for term limits despite holding his seat since 2001. He won reelection in 2024 with 59.5% of the vote, coasting on name recognition, defense industry cash, and AIPAC's generosity. When Wilson yelled "You lie!" he may have been projecting — because his voting record tells a very different story than his "supporting the troops" rhetoric.

Debbie Wasserman Schultz is a Democrat representing Florida's 25th Congressional District in southern Broward County, a seat she has held since 2005. A breast cancer survivor who championed the EARLY Act to promote cancer awareness among younger women [1], a former chair of the Democratic National Committee, and a senior member of the House Appropriations Committee [2], she is one of the most institutionally powerful Democrats in Florida. She is also, to put it delicately, a living case study in why Americans trust Congress about as much as they trust a gas station sushi bar. Her tenure as DNC chair ended in 2016 when leaked emails revealed staff bias favoring Hillary Clinton over Bernie Sanders, prompting her resignation on the eve of the Democratic convention [3] -- an episode that remains a landmark in the party's credibility deficit with its own base.

On the 80% Bill's twenty issues, Wasserman Schultz checks many of the expected Democratic boxes. She voted for the Inflation Reduction Act and its Medicare drug price negotiation provisions [4], has supported campaign finance reform rhetoric, and has generally aligned with her party on paid family leave, funding Social Security, and police accountability measures. She co-sponsored the For the People Act, which addresses gerrymandering and election reform [5]. On paper, she is the kind of Democrat who should be a natural champion for the 80% Bill's agenda. The trouble, as always with Wasserman Schultz, is what happens when you look past the paper.

The stock trading issue is where her record curdles. Wasserman Schultz has violated the STOCK Act -- the law requiring timely disclosure of congressional stock trades -- not once, not twice, but four documented times [6]. Her most recent violation, reported in July 2025, involved a trade in New Gold Inc. disclosed more than fourteen months late; the stock had tripled in value during the blackout period, netting a 464% profit while the public remained entirely in the dark [6]. For a lawmaker who ostensibly supports transparency and accountability, four violations of the one law designed to prevent congressional insider trading is not a rounding error -- it is a pattern. She has not cosponsored any of the leading bills to ban congressional stock trading outright, which tells you everything about where her actual interests lie.

Follow the money further and the contradictions deepen. Her top donor in the 2023-2024 cycle was AIPAC, at over $535,000, with large individual contributions comprising nearly 68% of her total fundraising [7]. As DNC chair, she reversed President Obama's ban on corporate lobbyist donations to the party [8], and she co-sponsored legislation to delay CFPB rules cracking down on predatory payday lenders -- earning the nickname "Debt Trap Debbie" -- before reversing course under withering public pressure [9]. She received approximately $68,000 from payday lending interests [9]. PAC contributions account for roughly 28% of her fundraising [7], making her theoretical support for banning corporate PACs precisely that: theoretical.

Wasserman Schultz is the kind of Democrat who makes it very difficult for Democrats to argue they are the party of reform. She will vote the right way on Medicare negotiation and paid family leave, but she will also trade stocks in secret, take half a million from AIPAC, shield payday lenders until the cameras turn on, and run the party machinery with her thumb on the scale. On the 80% Bill's core promise -- that popular reforms are being blocked by a system of legalized corruption -- she is less a counterexample than an exhibit.

Tim Walberg is what happens when you send a Baptist pastor to Congress and he stays for two decades. The longest-tenured member of Michigan's delegation, Walberg has represented the state's rural southern tier since 2007 — with a brief involuntary sabbatical in 2009-2010 when Democrat Mark Schauer unseated him — and currently serves his ninth term as the dean of Michigan's congressional caucus [1]. A product of Moody Bible Institute, Fort Wayne Bible College, and Wheaton College, Walberg's educational pedigree is a hat trick of evangelical academia, and his legislative career has been a faithful extension of its worldview: socially conservative, fiscally austere, and deeply skeptical of any government program that cannot be found in the Book of Leviticus [2].

On climate change, Walberg achieved a kind of viral infamy in 2017 when he told constituents that if global warming is real, "God will take care of it" — a theological delegation of responsibility that drew ridicule from environmentalists and theologians alike [3]. He has conceded that humans have "some impact" on the climate but maintains that mankind cannot "change the entire universe," a straw man so large it could itself contribute to greenhouse gas emissions. His lifetime score from the League of Conservation Voters hovers near single digits, and he has consistently opposed renewable energy mandates, emissions regulations, and the Paris Climate Agreement. On healthcare, he has voted repeatedly to repeal the Affordable Care Act and opposed the Respect for Marriage Act, which codified protections for interracial and same-sex marriages under federal law [4].

Walberg's most incendiary moment came in March 2024, when a constituent asked about U.S. humanitarian aid spending in Gaza. The former pastor responded that America "shouldn't be spending a dime on humanitarian aid" and that the situation "should be like Nagasaki and Hiroshima. Get it over quick" [5]. The Council on American-Islamic Relations called it "a clear call to genocide." Walberg later insisted he was using a "metaphor" about ending wars swiftly, a defense that satisfied almost nobody, given that the original metaphor involved the incineration of approximately 200,000 civilians. The comment drew condemnation across the political spectrum, though Walberg faced no formal censure and won reelection handily that November [6].

His commitment to the Trump wing of the Republican Party was cemented on January 6, 2021, when Walberg voted against certifying the electoral votes from Arizona and Pennsylvania, and had previously signed an amicus brief asking the Supreme Court to discard the presidential votes of four states, including his own [7]. That a sitting congressman from Michigan asked the courts to throw out Michigan's votes and then continued to represent Michigan without apparent irony is perhaps the most revealing detail of Walberg's career. He has also opposed the January 6th investigation committee and voted against the bipartisan commission to examine the Capitol attack. For a man who built his career on moral certainty, Walberg's record suggests that certainty bends reliably in whichever direction the party winds blow [8].

Sen. Peter Welch is the quiet workhorse of the Vermont delegation -- the guy who does the unsexy legislative grinding while Bernie gets the rallies and Balint gets the progressive caucus headlines. After 16 years in the House, Welch moved to the Senate in 2023 and immediately set about doing what he does best: introducing bipartisan bills that sound great in press releases and then vanish into committee.

Welch's crown jewel is prescription drug pricing. He co-led the SMART Prices Act with Amy Klobuchar to expand Medicare drug negotiation, partnered with Republican Josh Hawley on bipartisan drug pricing legislation, and helped push the Inflation Reduction Act's negotiation provisions into law. In October 2025, he introduced a bill with Wyden to block Republicans' "Big Pharma Bailout." On this plank of the 80% Bill, Welch is the real deal -- he's been fighting pharma pricing since his House days and has the legislative receipts to prove it.

On protecting farmland, Welch co-introduced the Farmland Access Act with Angus King to keep American farmland in agricultural use and expand access for the next generation. He also championed the FARM Act for right to repair, pushing for farmers' ability to fix their own tractors without begging John Deere for permission. "You buy the tractor, you should be able to repair it" is the kind of common-sense slogan that polls at about 90% -- which naturally means Congress hasn't passed it.

Then there's the campaign finance question. Welch pledged to refuse corporate PAC contributions for his Senate campaign, but as VTDigger documented, "trade associations don't count" in his definition. He accepted donations from the American Medical Association, National Association of Realtors, Council of Insurance Agents and Brokers, and the International Dairy Foods Association. During his House tenure, GE, Liberty Mutual, Toyota, Ford, and Home Depot all contributed. The semantic distinction between "corporate PAC" and "trade association PAC" is doing a lot of heavy lifting in that pledge.

On dark money, Welch co-signed a letter urging the IRS to restore disclosure requirements for tax-exempt political groups and championed the For the People Act. He talks a good game on transparency -- just don't look too closely at his own donor list.

Welch is a solid, if imperfect, ally of the 80% Bill agenda. He delivers on healthcare and agriculture but wobbles on the campaign finance reforms that would require him to actually change how he funds his own campaigns. In Vermont, that still makes him one of the better options -- which says more about the Senate than it does about Peter Welch.
